item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my reaction. "Growing building" has a tasty Series 1 vibe to it. "Growing building… but there's a narrative only tenuously connected and it's conveyed through a bunch of collapsibles" has a rancid Series 3 vibe. The writing was competent and the idea was quite good, but the injection of fighting canceled my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm thrilled by the biological analogue tree building concept, but found the logs disappointing. I was hoping for less AIs and interpersonal (intrapersonal?) disputes, more description of these "support systems"/what I was hoping/assuming would be organs. I'm not attached to these characters and I don't really care that they learned this week's kiddie show aesop by the end.

No-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't have the faintest idea what 'dust bunnies' were - at first I imagined them as small rabbits made from dust, but slowly I realised this could not be correct, and so I Googled it. We call them dustballs, which strikes me as a much more suitable term for an SCP document.

Right, that aside… I'm afraid I don't like this very much. I find the writing to be very clunky at all times (e.g. the very short sentences towards the beginning, the repetition of SCP-2555-1 in the third paragraph, the incorrect usage of the word 'subject' in the same paragraph), and there are also numerous grammatical mistakes. I corrected one of these and was going to correct more before realising quite how many there are. I get the impression that you're not a hugely experienced writer; that's absolutely fine, but I would recommend going through this with someone a bit more established to help you understand some of your mistakes, which, while minor, add up to a slightly awkward reading experience. I hope that doesn't sound too patronising.

Regarding the idea itself: I found it all right, but it didn't instantly grab me. I liked some of the mystery around it, but didn't like how little it was explored; I liked the idea of a power struggle and the strongly-worded emails, but not so keen on it degenerating into obviously human-like personalities; I like the general idea of a living building macro-organism, but I don't feel you took this idea anywhere, choosing instead to focus on the interaction between the quarrelling brothers (which is, by the way, one of the oldest stories in the book).

Also, one of the first things that occurred to me when learning that the dustclouds (there's another possible name for them) 'dissolve' their victims was that the Foundation wouldn't just send more people in after that without trying out various kinds of protective clothing. If the dust clumps can get through even the Foundation's toughest armour, that's pretty impressive, and surely worth a mention? I'd recommend, though, that they can't get through it (at least not immediately) and then you can have a more thorough and exciting exploration log.

I'd say my final verdict is that the germ of the idea is good, but you could do so much more with this that I'm downvoting in the hopes of encouraging a rewrite.

P.S. don't use brackets as much as me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the base idea, but I started to drift off on the part about the dust bunnies. I don't exactly know why I didn't like that small part, but it grew on me as I kept reading.

I was also a little put down with the last log. I share the same sentiment with PeppersGhost in that the sudden drama almost killed it for me. I was getting the impression that there was some sort of political or military force going on here, and was becoming quite fond of it, until the whole "brother" thing popped up.

Nonetheless, this intrigued me, so here's an upvote. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, I'm sure there is no clinical word for "dust bunny", unless I'm mistaken.

Google has failed me searching for a more clinical term for "Dust Bunny." Best I could find was "Dust Aggregate," which is more of a descriptor.

Fun fact- the term "dust bunny" has been used as an analogy for the creation of planetoids.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can understand your concerns.

-The basement areas are the only place of interest in the article. The rooms themselves are what you would normally expect from an office building. Originally, my description included a method in which the rooms themselves replicated like cells in the body. However, this didn't serve to enhance the article; It made the description long and boring. Additionally, my description of an event like this is limited by my current high school education, making it come off as amateur-ish. In the end, I decided to forego this and build upon the basements.

-Dust bunny monsters seems a bit of a cruel word from my perspective. But regardless, they serve as the immune system. The dust clumps together and form a big mass. If you were to brush it off, it would most likely reform around you.

Also, I'm sure there is no clinical word for "dust bunny", unless I'm mistaken.

- From my perspective, SCP-2555 is supposed to represent the word "living" in both a figurative and literal sense. It is figurative because you have two entities that are first robotic, but slowly become more human as you progress through the logs. Living is about being put in a predicament, struggling with said predicament, and then overcoming it and developing character. In a sense, these entities learn a "life lesson", if you will, that they need to grow up (hence the name of this SCP).

To conclude, I appreciate that you have taken your time to talk about why you disliked this article. To me, your opinion is immensely helpful in helping me to become a more effective writer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's taken me a while to put my finger on what it is I dislike about this. Ultimately, it's a few elements that bug me, which taken together make for kind of a clunky article in my reading:

  • Why are only the basement portions of the building explored? I get that the inner workings and the entities controlling the phenomenon are located here, but what is the rest of the building for? It bothers me that the question isn't even posed in the context of the article, which gives the impression that you didn't think about it, therefore lending this a sort of half-finished feel as I read it.
  • The dust bunny monsters don't sit well with me. Firstly, even in quotes I don't think the term "dust bunny" is going to show up in a clinical document. I'm also struggling to envision how these things are supposed to stop anyone. It's animate dust; how hard is it to just brush the stuff away?
  • The logs actually start out in an intriguing manner, but get less and less interesting to me as they devolve into silly bickering territory. This is sort of becoming a sub-genre around here; "sentient items that happen to sound like a twenty something human". I have to imagine that artificial intelligences are going to have a different enough perspective to have to at least struggle a bit to adopt a prosaic human attitude such as that displayed here. So it's a little immersion-breaking whenever I see this schtick pop up.

Taken together, this just doesn't work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My original back story was that this building was designed by an Architect with a vision. That vision is a mix between AWCY and the Venus Project.1

The man who designed this building wanted to show that humanity should take a direction in which our management of resources is self-sustaining. SCP-2555 as a standalone is completely self-sustaining. It gets renewable energy, absorbs nutrients from deep underground, and spits out its wastes back into the ground.

In addition, the two entities (Central and P-Guard) highlight the fact that the building is not just living, but is a complex organism, like most organisms are. The Architect put this in place to show this, but to also have a building that can think for itself and react in certain situations when needed be. After some time though, this Architect stepped out and allowed the two to figure things out on their own (which is the reason why they might feel some anger towards him. He is almost like a father to them).

I really wanted to include this, but I was struggling with how I could include this story element in without it being forced. Even if the Architect himself were to come back to check on the building once in a while, it just seems too much of a coincidence that the Foundation would stumble into him. Similarly, I tried to do a "note drop" in the form of a fancy plaque in the lobby. However, that quickly fell out of favor.

In the end, I decided to drop this. I felt having the conflict between the two entities as the highlight was more natural for me.

EDIT: Now that I think of it, I think this back story would be better expressed as a tale.

Footnotes
1. A real life organization that seeks to turn humanity towards a self-sustaining, resource-based system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why does he want them to control the building and grow? I get that a certain amount of vagueness is nice in any SCP, but this just makes me ask "why?" I totally understand that some SCPs don't need an answer to "why?" because they just are, but this one doesn't give me that vibe. I want to know more, but not in a good satisfying way. I feel like there's something missing.

I think the reason you're feeling that way is because there clearly is a "why," it just isn't logically suggested nor explicitly hinted at. This article "tells" quite a lot, so there's no sense that the SCP is ultimately beyond our comprehension or inherently mysterious. It's kind of mysterious just by omission, which results in that sense that something is missing.

To the author, if you're comfortable saying, I would be very interested in the backstory you conceived for this. Knowing that could help suggest some improvements, because while I do think it could use a little work, I also think it is interesting enough to merit doing that work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really want to know more.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It works on a lot of levels. It's weird, it's inexplicable, it's interesting, it hints at "more going on beneath the surface" without falling back on generic "oh it's really a threat!", it's dark and whimsical without cheating one for the other, and I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The whimsical outset belies how dark this really gets. Lots of interesting stuff going on under the surface, and I find I like the whole thing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was digging it up until the final collapsible. The sudden injection of family drama felt unnatural and out of place. +-0 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's definitely interesting. I like the idea of a building that not only grows, but houses two separate entities that control it. However, there's one that bothers me.

I like the arguing between the two brothers, however I don't know how I feel about them mentioning their father. Why does he want them to control the building and grow? I get that a certain amount of vagueness is nice in any SCP, but this just makes me ask "why?" I totally understand that some SCPs don't need an answer to "why?" because they just are, but this one doesn't give me that vibe. I want to know more, but not in a good satisfying way. I feel like there's something missing.

I probably missed something along the way, and I'll continue giving it another read, but for now I can't seem to vote either way. There's definitely a lot of good potential though, and you seem on the right track!

I like the individual basements, and how they get more complex as they go on. I do have a question though. Is the DNA sequence in B1 supposed to code for anything in particular, or is that just the buildings way of saying "yo, there's some new lifeforms up in here."?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The -1 are dust bunnies. (That is why I included the footnote to clear that up)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are they literally "dust bunnies", because the -1s seem kinda vague in description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was planning to do something like this a while ago, where a building grows at an exponential rate, but I think you did it better than I ever could. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure. I mean, I definitely like it. I'm just not clear on the motivation. That's ok, though. In my opinion, the ready holds itself up just fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you notice, the logs become less formal as they progress. B2 is minimal, almost robotic. B4 is less robotic, but still formal (almost business-like, if you will). By the final log, both sides are losing their cool until they eventually start acting like human beings. The conflict that they both have (which comes to light in the final log) brings them closer together.

This was just something new I was trying out (well, new for me). If anything else is unclear, feel free to ask.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's weird, and a little hard to follow at times. I like it, though. Pretty solid writing throughout. I'm still not sure that I /get it/, but I'm not sure that's important. +1

Edit: After another read through, I don't like the dust bunnies thing. I don't mind the idea as a "biological building has an immune response" thing, but I don't like that you /call/ them dust bunnies. Not enough for a no vote, but just my thoughts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, right. Thanks for clearing that up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, it was originally called Sustainable Future. (and is also under my name).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe you saw it on the drafts forum? Sustainable Future, I believe the thread was called.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it just me, or was this plagiarized?

Really, this is a genuine question, and I mean no disrespect by it. I think I've read this article before, but I can't remember. Maybe it got deleted and this is the repost, i'm not sure.

Now that that's cleared up, I can post my thoughts. Yay, individualism!

The concept on the whole is a little boring and cliche, but what made me give my upvote was that exchange between the two entities at the end. Especially:

No fair, you get the supply closets!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A little empty, and the idea is kind of hard to follow. No vote for now.
